pages.title: After your MSFH inspection report: first project checklist
pages.description: A practical checklist for turning the report into one clear first project and quote path.
pages.quickAnswer: Read the recommended improvements and cost sections first, then choose the first project before you collect broad quotes.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume the report alone means reimbursement is secure.
Do not assume the first contractor who mentions the program is the right next step.
Do not assume attached-home rules match detached-home scope.
keyTakeaways: The report is the roadmap, not the approval.
The first useful choice is usually between improvement paths, not between contractors.
Attached-home owners should branch early because scope can narrow.
nextSteps: Confirm what the recommendation actually says.
Choose the likely first project.
Collect the right quote checklist before you sign.

secondaryCta.label: Use the report to choose the first project

pages.title: Impact windows vs shutters after an MSFH report
pages.description: Use this guide when opening protection is recommended but the quote path is still unclear.
pages.quickAnswer: Do not choose between windows and shutters until you understand which openings the report is actually pushing you to protect.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume every opening must be replaced.
Do not assume a window-heavy sales pitch matches the report.
Do not assume attached homes can skip the scope check.
keyTakeaways: Opening protection is a category, not a single purchase.
Some homes need narrower scope than the first sales pitch suggests.
Attached-home owners should verify whether opening protection is the only grant-aligned path.
nextSteps: Read the opening-protection recommendation carefully.
Break out which openings need proof or pricing.
Use a quote checklist that matches the report.

secondaryCta.label: Go to the opening-protection decision page

relatedLinks.label: What the program may pay for

pages.title: MSFH quote signing checklist: questions to ask before you sign
pages.description: A stripped-down checklist for license fit, scope fit, timing, and permit handling before you sign.
pages.quickAnswer: Match the recommendation to the contractor type first, then verify license, permit responsibility, documentation, and scope before you treat the quote as safe.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume any quote mentioning the program is grant-aligned.
Do not assume permit closeout will take care of itself.
Do not assume a generic roofer and a retrofit specialist solve the same problem.
keyTakeaways: The homeowner still owns contractor choice.
The program does not maintain an authorized contractor list.
A clean quote explains the eligible scope instead of burying it.
nextSteps: Verify the recommendation category.
Check license fit and permit handling.
Separate eligible mitigation work from optional extras.

pages.title: Does your MSFH recommendation really require roof replacement?
pages.description: A broad guide for homeowners deciding whether roof replacement is truly required by the eligible mitigation path.
pages.quickAnswer: Roof replacement may fit only when it is required to complete the eligible recommendation. The quote should explain that link clearly before you budget around reimbursement.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume roof age alone creates eligibility.
Do not assume SWR language means every reroof line item is reimbursable.
Do not assume attached-home cases follow detached-home roof logic.
keyTakeaways: SWR logic matters more than generic roof age.
Eligible mitigation scope should stay visible in the quote.
Attached-home owners should verify scope before following roof-heavy messaging.
nextSteps: Confirm whether the report ties replacement to eligible work.
Review SWR and related roof recommendations.
Use a roofing quote checklist before signing.

secondaryCta.label: Check the narrow SWR exception page

relatedLinks.label: What the program may pay for

pages.title: Opening protection quote checklist after an MSFH recommendation
pages.description: Use this checklist to keep window, shutter, door, and garage-door quotes tied to the actual report scope.
pages.quickAnswer: Break the quote out by protected opening, license fit, permit handling, and documentation before you compare price.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume every opening needs the same product or the same scope.
Do not assume a window-heavy proposal matches the report recommendation.
Do not assume an attached home can drift into unrelated roof work just because the sales pitch widens.
keyTakeaways: Opening-protection quotes should stay opening-by-opening instead of broad and vague.
The contractor should separate recommended mitigation work from optional upgrades.
Attached-home cases should stay narrow before pricing expands.
nextSteps: Match each opening in the quote to the report recommendation.
Verify license fit, permit responsibility, and documentation handling.
Keep non-eligible upgrades separate before you sign.

secondaryCta.label: Return to the opening-protection decision page

relatedLinks.label: What the program may pay for

pages.title: Roof-to-wall quote checklist before you hire a retrofit specialist
pages.description: Use this checklist when a roof-to-wall recommendation needs a narrow retrofit quote instead of a broad roofing package.
pages.quickAnswer: Use a retrofit checklist before you accept any quote that turns a roof-to-wall recommendation into a general roofing package.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume a general roofer and a retrofit specialist solve the same problem.
Do not assume a broad reroof quote is required by a roof-to-wall recommendation.
Do not assume license fit is obvious from sales language alone.
keyTakeaways: Roof-to-wall work should stay tied to the report recommendation.
Retrofit license fit and scope detail matter more than a broad package price.
A quote should explain why any wider roof work is necessary.
nextSteps: Confirm the report points to roof-to-wall, not generic roof work.
Ask how the quoted scope completes the eligible retrofit.
Separate retrofit work from optional roofing extras before signing.

secondaryCta.label: Return to the roof-to-wall decision page

pages.title: SWR roof quote checklist before you approve broader roof work
pages.description: Use this checklist when an SWR recommendation is being used to justify broader roof scope or replacement.
pages.quickAnswer: If a quote expands into broader roof work, it should explain why that scope is necessary to complete the eligible SWR recommendation before you budget around reimbursement.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume SWR means blanket reroof approval.
Do not assume every roofing line item attached to the quote is reimbursable.
Do not assume the contractor has already separated eligible scope from optional extras.
keyTakeaways: SWR logic matters only when the broader roof scope is truly necessary.
A clean quote ties replacement language back to the report recommendation.
Work timing and approval still matter before the job starts.
nextSteps: Confirm the report actually points to SWR.
Ask which line items are required to complete the eligible mitigation work.
Separate grant-aligned scope from homeowner-choice roof upgrades.

primaryCta.label: Get the SWR roof routing checklist

secondaryCta.label: Return to the SWR exception route

relatedLinks.label: What the program may pay for

pages.title: Attached home under MSFH: when scope narrows to opening protection
pages.description: Use this guide when your home is attached or treated like a townhouse and the report path feels narrower than detached-home advice.
pages.quickAnswer: If your home is attached, stop before following roof-heavy advice. Current scope rules may narrow the first eligible path to opening protection.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume detached-home roof logic applies to your attached home.
Do not assume a contractor can widen scope just because the roof feels like the bigger project.
Do not assume attached-home rules disappear once a quote is in front of you.
keyTakeaways: Attached-home owners need a scope check earlier than most detached-home owners.
Opening protection may be the only clean first path even when roof questions feel urgent.
The safest quote path usually starts narrow and report-based.
nextSteps: Confirm whether the program is treating the home like a townhouse or other attached structure.
Read the recommendation before collecting broad roof quotes.
Use an opening-protection or project-choice route before you commit.

relatedLinks.label: What the program may pay for

pages.title: No recommended improvements on your MSFH report: what to do next
pages.description: Use this guide when the report does not recommend eligible work and you need the next move spelled out without drifting into generic contractor shopping.
pages.quickAnswer: If there are no recommended improvements, the grant path usually does not move forward. The right move is to confirm the report result, not to start buying work and hope it qualifies later.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume you can self-select a project and recover reimbursement later.
Do not assume a contractor can turn a no-recommendation report into an eligible grant path by itself.
Do not assume this is a pricing problem when it is really a recommendation problem.
keyTakeaways: No recommended improvements is usually a hard stop, not a shopping prompt.
This state needs clarification or a changed fact pattern before money should move.
The site should route you back to report confirmation, not into broad mitigation sales.
nextSteps: Re-check the report result and where the recommendation section points.
Use the support explanation route before collecting contractor quotes.
Only return to project-choice content if a real recommendation exists.

relatedLinks.label: What the program may pay for

pages.title: MSFH RFI response checklist before you sign or start work
pages.description: Use this checklist when an RFI is blocking the file and you need to clear the missing-information issue before treating the project as safe.
pages.quickAnswer: An RFI is a real block. Clear the missing-information request before you sign, start work, or budget around reimbursement.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume an RFI is minor enough to ignore while the project moves ahead.
Do not assume quote timing matters more than the unresolved file issue.
Do not assume the program will fix missing information after work starts.
keyTakeaways: RFI means the file is not clean enough yet.
The safest next step is corrective paperwork, not contractor acceleration.
Once the file clears, the user should return to the route that owns the actual project decision.
nextSteps: Identify exactly what information the file is missing.
Resolve the missing item before signing or starting work.
Return to the program route that matches the recommendation after the hold clears.

relatedLinks.label: Contractor quote route after the file clears
